directions.name: Rebalance PTX B Fragment Reuse Against Ps2r Feed Pressure
directions.hypothesis: Round 47's grouped-row increase from 4 to 8 already pushed the hot PTX band to a new best custom runtime of 25.529328 ms and cut DRAM throughput from 12.90% to 10.37% while lifting L2 throughput from 29.45% to 30.02%. That confirms block-order locality is still helping, but the remaining bottleneck has not moved to global memory. The active PTX microkernel still reloads the mirrored-column B fragment for every row-pair, which matches the still-elevated mio_throttle at 4.77% and suggests the next material win is to reduce shared-to-register feed pressure instead of chasing another small locality-only gain. Reintroducing a controlled B-fragment lookahead or a two-fragment rolling window in the PTX 128x128 path should cut Ps2r traffic without giving back the locality win from grouped-row=8.
directions.expected_bottleneck: Ps2r/shared-feed pressure in the PTX hot-band microkernel, showing up as elevated mio_throttle and underfed tensor issue despite lower DRAM demand.
directions.risk: Keeping more B fragments live can raise register pressure above the current 94 registers/thread and erase the gain by reducing latency hiding or perturbing the mirrored-column ownership. A careless rewrite can also reintroduce the longer export/live-range problem that the current PTX path was designed to trim.
metrics_to_recheck: median runtime and TFLOP/s against the 25.529328 ms best custom baseline
directions.stop_condition: Stop if mio_throttle does not fall meaningfully or if registers/thread rises enough to offset the feed improvement without a clear runtime win.

directions.name: Continue PTX Grouped-Row And Traversal Locality Tuning, But Treat It As Incremental
directions.hypothesis: The grouped-row separation and promotion to 8 clearly worked: runtime improved again, DRAM traffic dropped, L2 stayed slightly hotter, and both barrier and short-scoreboard stalls improved. That means the PTX block-order mapping is still a real lever. The right follow-up is not to abandon locality, but to scope it as a second-priority refinement: test whether the logical_pid remap can preserve the grouped-row=8 L2 benefit while improving CTA traversal inside each group, or whether a nearby grouping factor changes the L2/issue tradeoff. This is worth trying, but the current data says it is less likely than a Ps2r/feed fix to unlock the multi-millisecond gap to the sub-20 ms target.
directions.expected_bottleneck: Residual block-order locality inefficiency in the PTX hot-band traversal, now mostly visible as an incremental L2/DRAM opportunity rather than the dominant limiter.
directions.risk: Further grouping can hit diminishing returns quickly, hurt fairness across columns, or interact badly with the peeled 64x384 follow-on path. A more aggressive traversal may also preserve L2 locality while making shared-feed or synchronization slightly worse, which would look good on DRAM metrics but fail the <20 ms objective.
metrics_to_recheck: median runtime delta versus the grouped-row=8 baseline
directions.stop_condition: Stop if traversal tweaks only move DRAM/L2 counters while runtime gains stay marginal, because the user target is <20 ms rather than merely staying ahead of CUTLASS.

directions.name: Revisit PTX Hot-Band Stage Cadence And Sync Granularity
directions.hypothesis: Barrier stall improved from 7.63% to 7.18%, so the latest locality change did not worsen synchronization, but the PTX hot-band path still pays a full CTA sync around every K16 handoff. The non-PTX 128x128 branch still shows a two-K-tile cadence, while the active PTX path uses the restored K16 double buffer. If the B-fragment/feed rewrite does not clear the next bottleneck, the remaining opportunity is to retime the cp.async wait/commit cadence or selectively restore a larger stage cadence that lowers synchronization frequency without reviving the old shared/export pressure problem.
directions.expected_bottleneck: Synchronization and stage-transition overhead in the PTX hot-band loop, with barrier cost still material even after the grouped-row locality win.
directions.risk: Stage-cadence experiments can consume extra shared memory, lengthen live ranges, or bring back the very export/feed issues that earlier PTX simplifications removed. This is the highest-variance option and should trail the more directly evidenced Ps2r/feed work.
directions.stop_condition: Stop if barrier improves but mio_throttle or runtime regresses, because that means stage work is only moving pressure around instead of raising end-to-end throughput.

notes:
- Round 47 separated the active PTX grouped-row policy from 4 and tuned it to 8, improving runtime from 25.677312 ms to 25.529328 ms and setting another best custom result, so block-order locality is still real.
- Coalescing Access: grouped-row=8 already reduced DRAM pressure sharply, so pure coalescing is no longer the lead diagnosis.
- Data Reuse: confirmed positive signal, because L2 throughput rose from 29.45% to 30.02% while DRAM dropped from 12.90% to 10.37%.
- Async Copy: cp.async is still doing useful work, but the current wait/commit cadence remains tied to full CTA sync points, so async-copy retuning is secondary rather than first priority.
- Bank Conflict: the current profile does not present a strong bank-conflict spike, so a layout-only rewrite is not the first move this round.
- L2 Cache: still worth refining via grouped-row/traversal experiments, but likely incremental now that the main locality win is already harvested.
- Register Reuse: now more important, because the PTX microkernel explicitly shortened A/B live ranges by reloading B per row-pair; that may be too conservative after the locality improvement.
- Pg2s: global-to-shared feed looks healthier than before, so it is no longer the clearest blocker.
- Ps2r: remains the best match for the elevated 4.77% mio_throttle, making B fragment reuse and shared-feed reduction the highest-priority direction.
- Stage: still worth keeping as a third option because barrier fell only to 7.18%, not to a negligible level.
- Priority comparison for this round: 1) go back to PTX B fragment / Ps2r / shared-feed first, 2) continue grouped-row size or traversal tuning only as a secondary locality refinement, 3) revisit stage cadence or another sync-oriented variant only after the feed path is retested.
- Ranking is set against the actual user goal of <20 ms, not just the narrower goal of staying ahead of the local CUTLASS baseline.
